text: In this video, the ligation of the intersphincteric fistula tract procedure is performed. As you can see in this MRI, the patient has a transsphincteric fistula for which he has had cetan drainage for the last five months.

The surgeon administers a perianal nerve block to relax the sphincter and widen the intersphincteric plane. The intersphincteric plane is identified, and a probe is inserted into the fistula tract. The seton is removed, and the intersphincteric plane is opened using diathermy.
The surgeon identifies the fistula tract and isolates it within the intersphincteric plane. A probe remains fixated in the tract as careful dissection encircles the tract.

The surgeon removes the probe and ligates the fistula tract near the internal opening. The tract is then cut and sutured at the internal opening site. The matured fistula tract is visible as a fibrotic tube.

The surgeon checks the internal opening of the fistula tract to confirm closure after suturing. A probe is inserted to locate the fistula tract at the site of the external opening, which is then sutured.

The surgeon checks if the fistula tract is closed, then closes the wound with sutures. The external opening is excised and corrected, creating a fresh wound by excising the subcutaneous fistula tract up to the level of the external sphincter.
The surgeon completes the procedure by inserting paraffin gauze into the surgical site.
